title: Password Complexity & Reuse Audit Tool
description: A deep dive into auditing cracked credentials for password strength, complexity patterns, and reuse detection across enterprise environments.

title: Automating Cisco Configuration Audits
description: Strengthen your Cisco network by automating configuration audits with ease. A practical guide to security compliance checking.

title: FortiGate Configuration Files Audit Tool (FCFAT)
description: Enhancing firewall security with automated analysis of FortiGate configuration files for misconfigurations and compliance gaps.

content.text: Introduction

Conducting regular audits of firewall configurations is essential for ensuring compliance, identifying potential security gaps, and maintaining optimal performance. This is where theFortiGate Configuration Files Audit Tool(fcfat) comes into play.
content.html: Conducting regular audits of firewall configurations is essential for ensuring compliance, identifying potential security gaps, and maintaining optimal performance. This is where the FortiGate Configuration Files Audit Tool (fcfat) comes into play.

content.text: Developed to streamline the auditing process forFortinet FortiGate firewalls, this tool provides an automated way to analyze configuration files, detect misconfigurations, and generate actionable insights. In this blog post, we’ll explore the tool’s features, how it works, and how security teams can leverage it for effective firewall management.
content.html: Developed to streamline the auditing process for Fortinet FortiGate firewalls, this tool provides an automated way to analyze configuration files, detect misconfigurations, and generate actionable insights. In this blog post, we’ll explore the tool’s features, how it works, and how security teams can leverage it for effective firewall management.

content.text: Why Audit FortiGate Firewall Configurations?
Firewalls are complex systems with numerous rules, policies, and settings that can be difficult to manage manually. Common issues that arise due to misconfigurations include:
content.html: Firewalls are complex systems with numerous rules, policies, and settings that can be difficult to manage manually. Common issues that arise due to misconfigurations include:

items: Overly permissive rulesthat expose the network to unnecessary risks.
Unused or redundant policiesthat clutter configurations and reduce efficiency.
Non-compliant settingsthat violate security best practices or regulatory requirements.
Improper NAT and routing configurationsthat may disrupt network traffic.

content.text: Manual audits are time-consuming and prone to human error. Automating the auditing process helps organizations proactively secure their networks and meet compliance standards.
content.html: Manual audits are time-consuming and prone to human error. Automating the auditing process helps organizations proactively secure their networks and meet compliance standards.

content.text: Features of the FortiGate Configuration Files Audit Tool
This open-source tool simplifies the firewall auditing process by offering the following capabilities:
content.html: This open-source tool simplifies the firewall auditing process by offering the following capabilities:

items: Automated Configuration Analysis: Parses FortiGate configuration files and scans for security issues.
Rule and Policy Validation: Identifies redundant, conflicting, or overly permissive rules.
Security Best Practices Compliance: Ensures the configuration aligns with industry standards such asCIS benchmarks
Detailed Reporting: Generates reports highlighting critical findings and recommendations.
User-Friendly Interface: Provides a graphical and command-line interface for ease of use.
Standalone Executable: No need for additional dependencies or installations.
How the Tool Works
TheFortiGate Configuration Files Audit Toolis a Windows-based executable that processes configuration files exported from FortiGate firewalls. Below is a step-by-step guide on how to use it:
1. Download and Install
To get started, download the latest version of the tool from theGitHub repository

content.text: How the Tool Works
TheFortiGate Configuration Files Audit Toolis a Windows-based executable that processes configuration files exported from FortiGate firewalls. Below is a step-by-step guide on how to use it:
content.html: The FortiGate Configuration Files Audit Tool is a Windows-based executable that processes configuration files exported from FortiGate firewalls. Below is a step-by-step guide on how to use it:

content.text: 1. Download and Install
To get started, download the latest version of the tool from theGitHub repository
content.html: To get started, download the latest version of the tool from the GitHub repository

content.text: Benefits of Using This Tool
✅ Increased Security
By detecting misconfigurations and weak security settings, the tool helps strengthen firewall defenses against potential threats.
content.html: By detecting misconfigurations and weak security settings, the tool helps strengthen firewall defenses against potential threats.

content.text: ⏳ Time Efficiency
Automating the audit process reduces the time required to review configurations manually.
content.html: Automating the audit process reduces the time required to review configurations manually.

content.text: 📊 Improved Compliance
Ensures adherence to security frameworks such asISO 27001, NIST, and PCI DSS.
content.html: Ensures adherence to security frameworks such as ISO 27001, NIST, and PCI DSS.

content.text: 🛠 Actionable Insights
Provides clear and actionable recommendations to improve firewall configurations.
content.html: Provides clear and actionable recommendations to improve firewall configurations.

content.text: Future Enhancements
The tool is actively evolving, and future updates may include:
content.html: The tool is actively evolving, and future updates may include:

items: Integration with SIEM solutionsfor continuous monitoring.
Support for API-based auditsto analyze live configurations.
Enhanced visualization of security gapsthrough dashboards and graphs.

content.text: Conclusion

TheFortiGate Configuration Files Audit Toolis a powerful solution for automating firewall audits and ensuring that security policies remain robust and compliant. By leveraging this tool, IT security teams can proactively identify misconfigurations, enforce best practices, and enhance overall network security.
content.html: The FortiGate Configuration Files Audit Tool is a powerful solution for automating firewall audits and ensuring that security policies remain robust and compliant. By leveraging this tool, IT security teams can proactively identify misconfigurations, enforce best practices, and enhance overall network security.

content.text: 👉Get Started Now: Download the tool fromGitHuband start auditing your FortiGate firewalls today!
content.html: 👉 Get Started Now: Download the tool from GitHub and start auditing your FortiGate firewalls today!

content.text: Have feedback or feature suggestions? Contribute to the project on GitHub or share your insights in the comments below!
content.html: Have feedback or feature suggestions? Contribute to the project on GitHub or share your insights in the comments below!

title: Oracle EBS Security Auditing
description: An in-depth approach to auditing Oracle E-Business Suite, covering default credentials, weak passwords, application-level tests and database security controls.

content.text: So this is my attempt to improve on thispostI wrote last year and other tests that I find helpful. Whatever is outlined here really is a tip of the iceberg and further tests should definitely be done depending on scope, objectives…
content.html: So this is my attempt to improve on this post I wrote last year and other tests that I find helpful. Whatever is outlined here really is a tip of the iceberg and further tests should definitely be done depending on scope, objectives…

content.text: Application testing:
content.html: Application testing:

content.text: Controls to test:
content.html: Controls to test:

items: Default application account credentials
Weak application password controls
Poor patching policies
Directory listing / sensitive information exposure
Segregation of duties / roles and responsibilities
Etc

content.text: Default application account credentials:
content.html: Default application account credentials:

content.text: I have created a list of common default accounts on the application and uploaded ongithub, feel free to add to the list / ping me ontwitter. TBH, this issue is quite common among most instances I have the pleasure of auditing and mostly gives you system administrator privileges on the portal.
content.html: I have created a list of common default accounts on the application and uploaded on github, feel free to add to the list / ping me on twitter. TBH, this issue is quite common among most instances I have the pleasure of auditing and mostly gives you system administrator privileges on the portal.

content.text: Other tests to perform on the application to perhaps gain access arehere.
content.html: Other tests to perform on the application to perhaps gain access are here.

content.text: Solution:disable the default app accounts;
content.html: Solution:  disable the default app accounts;

content.text: Weak application password controls
content.html: Weak application password controls

content.text: Over and above the normal password complexity settings on the application, I found that if the passwords are not hashed and you have theAPPS password, one could decrypt ALL the application user passwords!!
content.html: Over and above the normal password complexity settings on the application, I found that if the passwords are not hashed and you have the APPS password, one could decrypt ALL the application user passwords!!

content.text: Lack of patching:
content.html: Lack of patching:

content.text: Simple select statement to view patch levels:
content.html: Simple select statement to view patch levels:

content.text: SELECTpatch_name, patch_type, maint_pack_level, creation_dateFROM applsys.ad_applied_patchesORDER BY creation_date DESC
content.html:
SELECT
patch_name
, patch_type
, maint_pack_level
, creation_date
FROM applsys.ad_applied_patches
ORDER BY creation_date DESC

content.text: Lack of patching on the app leads to many  OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities including but not limited to XSS, SQLis – most discovered by the guys below. A common example is the reflected XSS on the jtfLOVInProcess.jsp. Many other jsps on the EBS suffer from these issues. These include: ibeCZzpEntry.jsp, cskmrelstmts.jsp, cskmslctcat.jsp, cskmslctplat.jsp, csm5Sync.jsp, ibeCScdAgrmntDetail.jsp, ibutpqs.jsp, ieccaleassignexception.jsp and ieccaleexception.jsp
content.html: Lack of patching on the app leads to many  OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities including but not limited to XSS, SQLis – most discovered by the guys below. A common example is the reflected XSS on the jtfLOVInProcess.jsp. Many other jsps on the EBS suffer from these issues. These include: ibeCZzpEntry.jsp, cskmrelstmts.jsp, cskmslctcat.jsp, cskmslctplat.jsp, csm5Sync.jsp, ibeCScdAgrmntDetail.jsp, ibutpqs.jsp, ieccaleassignexception.jsp and ieccaleexception.jsp

content.text: Solution:Patch. Patch. Patch.
content.html: Solution: Patch. Patch. Patch.

content.text: Directory listing / sensitive information exposure
content.html: Directory listing / sensitive information exposure

content.text: See posthere
content.html: See post here

content.text: Solution:Reduce the attack surface by removing the JSPs not in use.
content.html: Solution: Reduce the attack surface by removing the JSPs not in use.

content.text: Big shoutout to the guys below for being ready to help the community in the ERP space:
content.html: Big shoutout to the guys below for being ready to help the community in the ERP space:

items: David Litchfield
Integrigy
Onapsis

title: Blackhat Europe 2017 – Conference Notes
description: Key takeaways from Blackhat Europe 2017 in London, with links to slide decks, videos, and tools from briefings and demonstrations.

content.text: I had the pleasure to attend theBlackhat Europe 2017in London – and it was enlightening! In this post, I shall provide links to the slide decks, videos and tools shared during the demonstrations, briefings and various talks. The abstracts for the briefings can be found on the official Blackhat Europe website.

title: From Shodan to Remote Code Execution #1 – Hacking Jenkins
description: Exploring how misconfigured Jenkins automation servers exposed on the internet can lead to full remote code execution.

content.text: In this era of extreme automation, whether for development, programming deployment or even security management are we getting closer to security maturity or are we better off without the automation?
content.html: In this era of extreme automation, whether for development, programming deployment or even security management are we getting closer to security maturity or are we better off without the automation?

content.text: In the next posts, I hope to uncover some of the tools/ applications employed on enterprises geared towards automation and better security but end up exposing and increasing surface area for attack. We start with Jenkins.
content.html: In the next posts, I hope to uncover some of the tools/ applications employed on enterprises geared towards automation and better security but end up exposing and increasing surface area for attack. We start with Jenkins.

content.text: Jenkins  is an open source automation server written in Java. Jenkins helps to automate the non-human part of software development process, with continuous integration and facilitating technical aspects of continuous delivery. It is a server-based system that runs in servlet containers such as Apache Tomcat. It supports version control tools, including AccuRev, CVS, Subversion, Git, Mercurial, Perforce, ClearCase and RTC, and can execute Apache Ant, Apache Maven and sbt based projects as well as arbitrary shell scripts and Windows batch commands.
content.html: Jenkins  is an open source automation server written in Java. Jenkins helps to automate the non-human part of software development process, with continuous integration and facilitating technical aspects of continuous delivery. It is a server-based system that runs in servlet containers such as Apache Tomcat. It supports version control tools, including AccuRev, CVS, Subversion, Git, Mercurial, Perforce, ClearCase and RTC, and can execute Apache Ant, Apache Maven and sbt based projects as well as arbitrary shell scripts and Windows batch commands.

content.text: It is used by several organizations globally and a quick Shodan search gives over 300,000 instances publicly accessible over the internet.
content.html: It is used by several organizations globally and a quick Shodan search gives over 300,000 instances publicly accessible over the internet.

content.text: We shift focus quickly to see if there are some portal instances on port 8081 and the figure goes down..
content.html: We shift focus quickly to see if there are some portal instances on port 8081 and the figure goes down..

content.text: Of these, some require credentials…..and some don’t 🙂 …some even have theManage Jenkinsoption which simply means among other things we are a somewhat privileged user (eg jenkins user) and can install plugins of choice.
content.html: Of these, some require credentials…..and some don’t 🙂 …some even have the Manage Jenkins option which simply means among other things we are a somewhat privileged user (eg jenkins user) and can install plugins of choice.

content.text: Most people I know would opt to install terminal plugin..for obvious reasons..
content.html: Most people I know would opt to install terminal plugin..for obvious reasons..

content.text: Running some commands.. :-O
content.html: Running some commands.. :-O

content.text: Enough!
content.html: Enough!

title: SAMBAry Save Us!! (CVE-2017-7494)
description: Exploiting the Samba remote code execution vulnerability (CVE-2017-7494), where all versions from 3.5.0 onwards were vulnerable to a malicious shared library upload attack.

content.text: Remember linux users laughing at Windows users because of the now all too famous Wannacry? Karma.
content.html: Remember linux users laughing at Windows users because of the now all too famous Wannacry? Karma.

content.text: According toSamba, “All versions of Samba from 3.5.0 onwards are vulnerable to a remote code execution vulnerability, allowing a malicious client to upload a shared library to a writable share, and then cause the server to load and execute it.” Might appear not to be serious,but a quick look at our beloved Shodan we see roughlyhalfa millioncompanies with publicly exposed samba (shares.) – not to say all are exploitable but narrowing down and just by viewing the versions, more than half are!!!
content.html: According to Samba, “All versions of Samba from 3.5.0 onwards are vulnerable to a remote code execution vulnerability, allowing a malicious client to upload a shared library to a writable share, and then cause the server to load and execute it.” Might appear not to be serious,but a quick look at our beloved Shodan we see roughly half a million companies with publicly exposed samba (shares.) – not to say all are exploitable but narrowing down and just by viewing the versions, more than half are!!!

content.text: Exploitation:
content.html: Exploitation:

content.text: Various exploits have been released, I explored the exploit by HD Moore and it works pretty well; At the time of writing it had not been ported to metasploit, port it manually as below, fire up msf.
content.html: Various exploits have been released, I explored the exploit by HD Moore and it works pretty well; At the time of writing it had not been ported to metasploit, port it manually as below, fire up msf.

content.text: We would all appreciate a Check module in the msf exploit, since it is not there, I decided to do a manual grep – tedious if you a have a large estate to check but better safe than sorry.
content.html: We would all appreciate a Check module in the msf exploit, since it is not there, I decided to do a manual grep – tedious if you a have a large estate to check but better safe than sorry.

content.text: How to check:
content.html: How to check:

content.text: Download either ofthese files, which show open ports 139 and 445 for public IPs globally as at May 24th and search for your IP of interest :-)..am sure programmers have an easier way?
content.html: Download either of these files, which show open ports 139 and 445 for public IPs globally as at May 24th and search for your IP of interest :-)..am sure programmers have an easier way?

content.text: Remediation:
content.html: Remediation:

content.text: As usual PATCH! Samba have already released apatchfor versions 4.4 onwards. There are workarounds for the other versions, though they could break functionality – the workaround is to add the parameter “nt pipe support = no” to the smb.conf global section and restart smbd service.
content.html: As usual PATCH! Samba have already released a patch for versions 4.4 onwards. There are workarounds for the other versions, though they could break functionality – the workaround is to add the parameter “nt pipe support = no” to the smb.conf global section and restart smbd service.

content.text: On a light note 😀
content.html: On a light note 😀

content.html: Post title inspired by Remy Zero’s Save me…

content.html: A sequel to the last post of what is now becoming a series of “From Shodan to remote code execution”, we now take a look at how to hack misconfigured Dreambox installations. Dreambox is a company which offers Digital TV set top boxes and other related services.

title: Exploiting Windows with Eternalblue & Doublepulsar with Metasploit
description: Hands-on walkthrough of the NSA's EternalBlue and DoublePulsar exploits using Metasploit, the tools behind the WannaCry outbreak.

content.text: Most of us got hold of the NSA exploits recently released to the public and there was so much hype and public statements around it. A lot has been said, and most vendors came out to defend their products and to release patches to downplay/mitigate the impact of these exploits.
content.html: Most of us got hold of the NSA exploits recently released to the public and there was so much hype and public statements around it. A lot has been said, and most vendors came out to defend their products and to release patches to downplay/mitigate the impact of these exploits.

content.text: In the exploits, we came to learn about Fuzzbunch, NSA’s exploit framework – “NSA’s metasploit”. I know a few people who have tried to use it and fail due to lack of knowledge/ familiarity with the dependencies that Fuzzbunch demands…fret no more. We can exploit the same vulnerabilities using our beloved Metasploit :-). Currently Metasploit has the MS17-010 SMB RCE Detection module which “uses information disclosure to determine if MS17-010 has been patched or not. Specifically, it connects to the IPC$ tree and attempts a transaction on FID 0. If the status returned is “STATUS_INSUFF_SERVER_RESOURCES”, the machine does not have the MS17-010 patch. If the machine is missing the MS17-010 patch, the module will check for an existing DoublePulsar (ring 0 shellcode/malware) infection. This module does not require valid SMB credentials in default server configurations. It can log on as the user “\” and connect to IPC$.” It is a scanner module and just informs you that you need to patch your windows boxes – extremely useful for blue teamers.
content.html: In the exploits, we came to learn about Fuzzbunch, NSA’s exploit framework – “NSA’s metasploit”. I know a few people who have tried to use it and fail due to lack of knowledge/ familiarity with the dependencies that Fuzzbunch demands…fret no more. We can exploit the same vulnerabilities using our beloved Metasploit :-). Currently Metasploit has the MS17-010 SMB RCE Detection module which “uses information disclosure to determine if MS17-010 has been patched or not. Specifically, it connects to the IPC$ tree and attempts a transaction on FID 0. If the status returned is “STATUS_INSUFF_SERVER_RESOURCES”, the machine does not have the MS17-010 patch. If the machine is missing the MS17-010 patch, the module will check for an existing DoublePulsar (ring 0 shellcode/malware) infection. This module does not require valid SMB credentials in default server configurations. It can log on as the user “\” and connect to IPC$.” It is a scanner module and just informs you that you need to patch your windows boxes – extremely useful for blue teamers.

content.text: If you are interested to exploit, read on…
content.html: <del>If you are interested to exploit, read on…</del>

content.text: Our friends atEleven Paths, created a Metasploit module that we can add to our MSF and get a nice meterpreter session.
content.html: Our friends at Eleven Paths, created a Metasploit module that we can add to our MSF and get a nice meterpreter session.

content.text: Steps:
content.html: <del>Steps:</del>

items: Download the file from https://github.com/ElevenPaths/Eternalblue-Doublepulsar-Metasploit
Copy theeternalblue_doublepulsar.rbfile to the metasploit windows smb exploits path (/usr/share/metasploit-framework/modules/exploits/windows/smb)
Load msf and select the eternalblue_doublepulsar module

content.html: <del>At this point it is important to remind ourselves what the two exploits really do:</del>

content.html: <del>and the parameters we need to input:</del>

content.text: And with that,we are set. Fire up the exploit…and voila! Meterpreter session as SYSTEM – we do not even need to elevate privileges :-O
content.html: <del>And with that,we are set. Fire up the exploit…and voila! Meterpreter session as SYSTEM – we do not even need to elevate privileges :-O</del>

content.text: Though Microsoft hasreleasedpatches for this issue, there are millions if not billions of servers, workstations still vulnerable for various reasons. And worse, it is now easier to exploit, as we have seen.
content.html: Though Microsoft has released patches for this issue, there are millions if not billions of servers, workstations still vulnerable for various reasons. And worse, it is now easier to exploit, as we have seen.

title: Penetration Testing Sharepoint
description: Reconnaissance and security testing techniques for Microsoft Sharepoint, using Google dorks and OWASP Top 10 vulnerability checks.

content.text: Like any normal web application, Sharepoint may fall prey to OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities with a special focus on XSS, mostly due to inadequate patching and misconfiguration. On this post, we focus on recon / what sharepoint is exposing.
content.html: Like any normal web application, Sharepoint may fall prey to OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities with a special focus on XSS, mostly due to inadequate patching and misconfiguration. On this post, we focus on recon / what sharepoint is exposing.

title: Word Heist!
description: Leveraging MS Word documents for social engineering, capturing NTLM hashes without macros using a clever spear-phishing technique.

content.text: So, I stumbled upon an interesting script.
content.html: So, I stumbled upon an interesting script.

content.text: Over the years, I have been using various tools and scripts to do spear phishing; with the many vulnerabilities in Microsoft Office Suite and Adobe PDF reader being enablers and the ability to embed macros being an even bigger enabler. But things have changed and users are now keen on the “enable macro warning” and they don’t enable macros anymore 😦
content.html: Over the years, I have been using various tools and scripts to do spear phishing; with the many vulnerabilities in Microsoft Office Suite and Adobe PDF reader being enablers and the ability to embed macros being an even bigger enabler. But things have changed and users are now keen on the “enable macro warning” and they don’t enable macros anymore 😦

content.text: …hence most spear phishing attempts fail or are filtered by mail filters, firewalls, IPSs as they are deemed suspicious.
content.html: …hence most spear phishing attempts fail or are filtered by mail filters, firewalls, IPSs as they are deemed suspicious.

content.text: Sothiswas a breath of fresh air and seems to get through most IPSs and security devices.
content.html: So this was a breath of fresh air and seems to get through most IPSs and security devices.

content.text: On your listening server:
content.html: On your listening server:

content.text: Download thescript. Put an image of your choice in the folder. I chose elephant.jpg
content.html: Download the script. Put an image of your choice in the folder. I chose elephant.jpg

content.text: Start script as below. For this we want to set this server as a listening server hence the1after the image. Setting to 0 will not run the listener.
content.html: Start script as below. For this we want to set this server as a listening server hence the 1 after the image. Setting to 0 will not run the listener.

content.text: The script is mostly automated and the onlyhardwork is to send the file. Notice below that an *.rtf file (1491333207.rtf) has been generated from the *.jpg (elephant.jpg) image.
content.html: The script is mostly automated and the only hard work is to send the file. Notice below that an *.rtf file (1491333207.rtf) has been generated from the *.jpg (elephant.jpg) image.

content.text: This is what we need to send to our victim, and when we need to get really creative..
content.html: This is what we need to send to our victim, and when we need to get really creative..

content.text: After sending the *.rtf document to victim:
content.html: After sending the *.rtf document to victim:

content.text: Wait for victim to open the document, and immediately you notice NTLM hashes being populated on the listening server terminal. At the same time a file,passwords_netntlmv2,which contains NTLMv2 hashesis created. Which brings us to the last step – cracking the hashes..
content.html: Wait for victim to open the document, and immediately you notice NTLM hashes being populated on the listening server terminal. At the same time a file, passwords_netntlmv2, which contains NTLMv2 hashes is created. Which brings us to the last step – cracking the hashes..

content.text: Password cracking
content.html: Password cracking

content.text: There are many tools to do this, but john the ripper is recommended for various reasons. I used JTR for this illustration as below:
content.html: There are many tools to do this, but john the ripper is recommended for various reasons. I used JTR for this illustration as below:

content.text: And voila, passwords of the victim!
content.html: And voila, passwords of the victim!

content.text: Note:it would have definitely taken a longer time depending on the complexity of the password and wordlist used.
content.html: Note: it would have definitely taken a longer time depending on the complexity of the password and wordlist used.

content.text: From a blue teamers point of view:
content.html: From a blue teamers point of view:

items: We see the need for a strong password which should meet password complexity requirements
The back connection via port 445 is common especially for meterpreter sessions and this would point, though not necessarily, to an indicator of compromise.

title: Do You Know What Your ERP Is Telling Us?
description: Auditing Oracle E-Business Suite from an insider threat and external attacker perspective, uncovering information disclosure in ERP systems.

content.text: Interesting engagement I had a few weeks ago, a client wanted assurance on their ERP – Oracle E-Business suite, to be specific. I spent a few weeks just to formulate an efficient strategy and be able to cover most controls from an insider threat perspective and an external authenticated attacker angle.
content.html: Interesting engagement I had a few weeks ago, a client wanted assurance on their ERP – Oracle E-Business suite, to be specific. I spent a few weeks just to formulate an efficient strategy and be able to cover most controls from an insider threat perspective and an external authenticated attacker angle.

content.text: For this post, I shall focus on an external unauthenticated attacker angle with a bias to information disclosure, hence the title. No intrusion – give us consent to your environment and I shall be happy to demo. 🙂
content.html: For this post, I shall focus on an external unauthenticated attacker angle with a bias to information disclosure, hence the title. No intrusion – give us consent to your environment and I shall be happy to demo. 🙂

content.text: The Oracle EBS suite is a pretty massive estate – version 11 for instance is reported to have around 15000 JSP files, 800 enabled PL/SQL packages and procedures,countless forms, countless servlets etc. This, ofcourse, increases the attack surface considerably: attackers are happy, blue teamers should be worried.
content.html: The Oracle EBS suite is a pretty massive estate – version 11 for instance is reported to have around 15000 JSP files, 800 enabled PL/SQL packages and procedures,countless forms, countless servlets etc. This, ofcourse, increases the attack surface considerably: attackers are happy, blue teamers should be worried.

content.text: Before we dive in to Oracle EBS, I should say SAP also has its own vulnerabilities too, which I should cover in coming weeks especially around permissions, storage of passwords and use of default user credentials –SAP, DDIC, EARLYWATCH, anyone?
content.html: Before we dive in to Oracle EBS, I should say SAP also has its own vulnerabilities too, which I should cover in coming weeks especially around permissions, storage of passwords and use of default user credentials –SAP, DDIC, EARLYWATCH, anyone?

content.text: What info can we get from Oracle EBS version 11 as an external unauthenticated user?
content.html: What info can we get from Oracle EBS version 11 as an external unauthenticated user?

items: Log files:

content.text: What do most log files usually have? username, source IP/name, destination IP/name, protocols in use, descriptive error messages, service name/properties, right? What if I told you can get all that as an unauthenticated external user?
content.html: What do most log files usually have? username, source IP/name, destination IP/name, protocols in use, descriptive error messages, service name/properties, right? What if I told you can get all that as an unauthenticated external user?

content.text: Focus on the blurred parameters and the framed items in the screenshot. Lots of (useful) information, don’t you think?
content.html: Focus on the blurred parameters and the framed items in the screenshot. Lots of (useful) information, don’t you think?

content.text: 2.Upload folder
content.html: 2. Upload folder

content.text: As an unauthenticated user i.e. guest user one is able to reach an upload page…
content.html: As an unauthenticated user i.e. guest user one is able to reach an upload page…

content.text: Looking closely, we can see that we are authenticated.. It would interest you to find out what you can upload and how it impacts the security of your EBS suite. As earlierpromised?, I won’t delve much into the exploitation, but rather focus on the amount of information an unauthenticated attacker has access to.
content.html: Looking closely, we can see that we are authenticated.. It would interest you to find out what you can upload and how it impacts the security of your EBS suite. As earlier promised?, I won’t delve much into the exploitation, but rather focus on the amount of information an unauthenticated attacker has access to.

content.text: 3.Cookie properties:
content.html: 3. Cookie properties:

content.text: Any attacker loves cookies. Main reason: they hold keys to sessions:
content.html: Any attacker loves cookies. Main reason: they hold keys to sessions:

content.text: 4.Create form:
content.html: 4. Create form:

content.text: Want to create a form? As an unauthenticated user? No problem
content.html: Want to create a form? As an unauthenticated user? No problem

content.text: 5.Status of servlets:
content.html: 5. Status of servlets:

content.text: Want to know version of servlets and if they are working? No problem!
content.html: Want to know version of servlets and if they are working? No problem!

content.text: 6.A few configuration files:
content.html: 6.  A few configuration files:

content.text: 7.Version disclosure:
content.html: 7. Version disclosure:

content.text: 8.Diagnostics page:
content.html: 8. Diagnostics page:

content.text: A cool diagnostics page to get even more information 🙂
content.html: A cool diagnostics page to get even more information 🙂

content.text: 8.Username and Passwords:
content.html: 8. Username and Passwords:

content.text: Saving the best for last – username and passwords!!!!!!
content.html: Saving the best for last – username and passwords!!!!!!

content.text: The above passwords are SHA-1 encrypted and are easily decrypted. As a matter of fact the above are the default passwords shipped with the Oracle installation.
content.html: The above passwords are SHA-1 encrypted and are easily decrypted. As a matter of fact the above are the default passwords shipped with the Oracle installation.

content.text: Remember this is just got by sampling some of the JSPs, servlets and forms. It is not realistic to scope in an engagement for all the JSPs as an external infosec consultant. As a resident infosec engineer, I would be keen on all the services, JSPs, servlets, forms etc. Remember:attackers need to get it right onlyonce, blue team needs to get it rightALLthe time.
content.html: Remember this is just got by sampling some of the JSPs, servlets and forms. It is not realistic to scope in an engagement for all the JSPs as an external infosec consultant. As a resident infosec engineer, I would be keen on all the services, JSPs, servlets, forms etc. Remember: attackers need to get it right only once, blue team needs to get it right ALL the time.

content.text: Also, keep in mind while doing a security audit for an ERP, focus is how much data can we get and not necessarily an OS shell.
content.html: Also, keep in mind while doing a security audit for an ERP, focus is how much data can we get and not necessarily an OS shell.

content.text: This post wouldn’t be complete without some mitigating controls:
content.html: This post wouldn’t be complete without some mitigating controls:

items: Reduce attacker surface by removing the JSPs, servlets, services, forms not in use. This should be a well planned operation and involvement from the business is key. Trust me you should be able to reduce attack surface by up to 95%. I would be happy to hearfeedbackon this..:-)
Apply periodic Oracle EBS critical patch updates CPUs – this is very key.

Block access to links that an unauthenticated user wouldn’t need access to – you could start with the ones demonstrated in this post and others that you discover; create a custom 404 page to redirect traffic to these pages.
Review access logs and disable unnecessary access

content.text: I should be putting up another post on the EBS version 12 pretty soon…cheers!
content.html: I should be putting up another post on the EBS version 12 pretty soon…cheers!

title: Auditing Linux/Unix OS in 120 Seconds Flat
description: A baseline security auditing script for Linux and Unix operating systems, modelled around CIS benchmark controls that runs in under 2 minutes.

content.text: Well, most of us have seen the movie Gone in 60 seconds, so I decided to write a baseline script for auditing linux and most unix operating systems in well under 2 mins – averages about  130 seconds on my test Centos and Red hat distributions.
content.html: Well, most of us have seen the movie Gone in 60 seconds, so I decided to write a baseline script for auditing linux and most unix operating systems in well under 2 mins – averages about  130 seconds on my test Centos and Red hat distributions.

content.text: The script is modeled around most of the operating system controls as recommended by theCenter of Internet Security.
content.html: The script is modeled around most of the operating system controls as recommended by the Center of Internet Security.

content.text: What I really like about the script is the ease of downloading, using it and customizing it to fit your security risk appetite, and operating system flavour.
content.html: What I really like about the script is the ease of downloading, using it and customizing it to fit your security risk appetite, and operating system flavour.

title: Huawei HG8245H Router "Privilege Escalation": Part I
description: Exploring the Huawei HG8245H home router, from default credentials to privilege escalation and full configuration extraction.

content.text: This is a prequel to thispost here
content.html: This is a prequel to this post here

content.text: Well, I got to play around with my router a few weeks ago. My router, a Huawei HG8245H version, is pretty decent for home use.
content.html: Well, I got to play around with my router a few weeks ago. My router, a Huawei HG8245H version, is pretty decent for home use.

content.text: First things first, the login password is smack on the bottom of router as below.
content.html: First things first, the login password is smack on the bottom of router as below.

content.text: Most routers have a well known default password, infact there are multiple sites dedicated to documentthat. So I got curious to know what info I could get using these credentials.
content.html: Most routers have a well known default password, infact there are multiple sites dedicated to document that. So I got curious to know what info I could get using these credentials.

content.text: A quick google search says that the user root/admin is a normal user with the telecomadmin/admintelecom being the super user. Funny enough, I was unable to log in using the admintelecom/telecomadmin set of credentials. The superuser account allows a user tohave access to other options, notably backup configuration settings, edit and load router config file etc.
content.html: A quick google search says that the user root/admin is a normal user with the telecomadmin/admintelecom being the super user. Funny enough, I was unable to log in using the admintelecom/telecomadmin set of credentials. The superuser account allows a user to have access to other options, notably backup configuration settings, edit and load router config file etc.

content.text: An explanation I got as to why this is the case is because as soon as the router gets connected to ISP WAN it grabs configuration from ISP and this particular set of admin credentials don’t work. So how do we bypass this?
content.html: An explanation I got as to why this is the case is because as soon as the router gets connected to ISP WAN it grabs configuration from ISP and this particular set of admin credentials don’t work. So how do we bypass this?

content.text: Proof of concept:
content.html: Proof of concept:

items: Enter web interface (http://192.168.100.1) using root/admin credentials
Reboot the router.
Disconnect fibre cable as it restarts
As it restarts, try to log in onhttp://192.168.100.1as telecomadmin/admintelecom

content.text: Voila! You are in, as superadmin, with more optionsto tweak the router 😀
content.html: Voila! You are in, as superadmin, with more options to tweak the router 😀

content.text: 5. To elevate your normal user root to superadmin status. Download router config file           from System Tools > Configuration File.. This  file named “hw_ctree.xml” is                             encrypted and appears as below:
content.html: 5. To elevate your normal user root to superadmin status. Download router config file           from System Tools > Configuration File.. This  file named “hw_ctree.xml” is                             encrypted and appears as below:

content.text: Fortunately, we can decrypt the router config file using any aes decryption tool.
content.html: Fortunately, we can decrypt the router config file using any aes decryption tool.

content.text: 6. Proceed to decrypt as below:
content.html: 6. Proceed to decrypt as below:

content.text: and here we have our plaintext config file:
content.html: and here we have our plaintext config file:

content.text: For this post, our area of concern would be the part highlighted below:
content.html: For this post, our area of concern would be the part highlighted below:

content.text: Notice the different userlevels for the two users (root and telecomadmin), 0 and 1. Now we know userlevel 0 is superadministrator.
content.html: Notice the different userlevels for the two users (root and telecomadmin), 0 and 1. Now we know userlevel 0 is superadministrator.

content.text: 7. Edit the root user line to userlevel 0. Save file and decrypt it
content.html: 7. Edit the root user line to userlevel 0. Save file and decrypt it

content.text: 8. Log in to our web interface, upload the new config file and restart router.
content.html: 8. Log in to our web interface, upload the new config file and restart router.

content.text: 9.Once restarted, log in as root/admin, and enjoy the new options available  🙂
content.html: 9.Once restarted, log in as root/admin, and enjoy the new options available  🙂

content.text: I called up Huawei to notify them of this and after a rather lengthy discussion they finally emailed me:“We will not track this issue as a vulnerability. If you still have some different option please never hesitate to contact us.Thanks again for your concern about the security problems of Huawei products. If you ever find any potential security issues in Huawei products in the future, we are looking forward to working with you again.”
content.html: I called up Huawei to notify them of this and after a rather lengthy discussion they finally emailed me: “We will not track this issue as a vulnerability. If you still have some different option please never hesitate to contact us. Thanks again for your concern about the security problems of Huawei products. If you ever find any potential security issues in Huawei products in the future, we are looking forward to working with you again.”

content.text: I would,however like to thank Huawei’s quick response and follow up on their part. Many security researchers would have however have wished that we would fix this issue as we all know how attacks like DDOS are beingpropagatedusing default credentials in routers or other IOT devices.
content.html: I would,however like to thank Huawei’s quick response and follow up on their part. Many security researchers would have however have wished that we would fix this issue as we all know how attacks like DDOS are being propagated using default credentials in routers or other IOT devices.

title: Lateral Movement: Part I
description: How a normal domain user with no admin privileges can exploit Group Policy Preferences (GPP) passwords to become local administrator across the organisation.
